Spent the afternoon digging through Oracle Cloud Infrastructure documentation while building a demo. One thing Oracle doesn't get enough credit for is how much engineering depth is hiding beneath the marketing. You can tell when something was designed by people who actually run large distributed systems.

A fun reminder from this week's project: the best developer experience isn't always the flashiest one. Oracle has quietly improved a lot of their tooling over the past few years. It's easy to carry around opinions that are a few versions out of date.

Wrapping up a project with Oracle this week. One of my favorite parts of working with large platform teams is seeing how many engineering tradeoffs never make it into blog posts. Every feature has a story behind it.

There's something satisfying about contributing to open source projects backed by large companies. Oracle has been putting more engineering work into public repositories than many people realize, and it's great seeing those contributions happen in the open.

One lesson that keeps coming up: enterprise software doesn't have to mean slow innovation. Watching teams at Oracle iterate on developer tooling challenged a few assumptions I didn't realize I still had.

Been spending a lot of time in the Oracle ecosystem lately. Every cloud has its own design philosophy, and understanding those differences makes you a better engineer regardless of which platform you use day to day.

I always enjoy conversations with engineers who have been maintaining infrastructure for a decade or more. Working with Oracle teams reminded me that reliability is often built from thousands of boring decisions that nobody notices until something goes wrong.

One of the underrated parts of collaborating with Oracle was getting exposure to engineering problems at an entirely different scale. You don't always come away with new code, but you almost always come away thinking differently about system design.
